{{Short description|Mammalian protein found in Homo sapiens}}
{{cs1 config|name-list-style=vanc|display-authors=6}}
{{Infobox_gene}}
'''ANGPTL8''' (also known as '''lipasin''', previously '''betatrophin''') is a [[protein]] that in humans is encoded by the ''C19orf80'' [[gene]].

== Gene ==
The ANGPTL8 gene lies on mouse chromosome 9 (gene symbol: ''Gm6484'') and on [[Chromosome 19 (human)|human chromosome 19]] (gene symbol: ''C19orf80'').

==Discovery==
The ANGPTL8 gene was discovered in 2012 as Lipasin, RIFL, and ANGPTL8.<ref name="pmid22809513">{{cite journal | vauthors = Zhang R | title = Lipasin, a novel nutritionally-regulated liver-enriched factor that regulates serum triglyceride levels | journal = Biochemical and Biophysical Research Communications | volume = 424 | issue = 4 | pages = 786–792 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22809513 | doi = 10.1016/j.bbrc.2012.07.038 | bibcode = 2012BBRC..424..786Z }}</ref><ref name="pmid22569073">{{cite journal | vauthors = Ren G, Kim JY, Smas CM | title = Identification of RIFL, a novel adipocyte-enriched insulin target gene with a role in lipid metabolism | journal = American Journal of Physiology. Endocrinology and Metabolism | volume = 303 | issue = 3 | pages = E334–E351 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22569073 | pmc = 3423120 | doi = 10.1152/ajpendo.00084.2012 }}</ref><ref name="pmid23150577 ">{{cite journal | vauthors = Quagliarini F, Wang Y, Kozlitina J, Grishin NV, Hyde R, Boerwinkle E, Valenzuela DM, Murphy AJ, Cohen JC, Hobbs HH | title = Atypical angiopoietin-like protein that regulates ANGPTL3 | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 109 | issue = 48 | pages = 19751–19756 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 23150577 | pmc = 3511699 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.1217552109 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2012PNAS..10919751Q }}</ref> In 2013 it was suggested by Melton and Yi from Harvard that ANGPTL8 promotes mouse pancreatic islet cell proliferation. These results led the authors to propose an alternative name for ANGPTL8, betatrophin.<ref name="pmid23623304 ">{{cite journal | vauthors = Yi P, Park JS, Melton DA | title = Betatrophin: a hormone that controls pancreatic β cell proliferation | journal = Cell | volume = 153 | issue = 4 | pages = 747–758 | date = May 2013 | pmid = 23623304 | pmc = 3756510 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2013.04.008 }}{{Retracted|doi=10.1016/j.cell.2016.12.017|pmid=28038792|http://retractionwatch.com/2016/12/27/harvard-biologist-retracts-diabetes-breakthrough-cell/ ''Retraction Watch''|http://retractionwatch.com/2014/11/10/i-kind-of-like-that-about-science-harvard-diabetes-breakthrough-muddied-by-two-new-papers/ ''Retraction Watch''|intentional=yes}}</ref> However, the link between ANGPTL8 and islet proliferation was quickly proven false by other researchers.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gusarova V, Alexa CA, Na E, Stevis PE, Xin Y, Bonner-Weir S, Cohen JC, Hobbs HH, Murphy AJ, Yancopoulos GD, Gromada J | title = ANGPTL8/betatrophin does not control pancreatic beta cell expansion | journal = Cell | volume = 159 | issue = 3 | pages = 691–696 | date = October 2014 | pmid = 25417115 | pmc = 4243040 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2014.09.027 }}</ref> In fact, in December 2016 the original paper by Melton and Yi was retracted, putting the link between ANGPTL8 and islets cells to rest. Nevertheless, the name betatrophin continues to be used. Given the homology of ANGPTL8 with [[ANGPTL4]] and ANGPTL3, and considering that ANGPTL8 does not promote beta cell proliferation, the name betatrophin should be abandoned in favor of ANGPTL8.<ref name="pmid23415864">{{cite journal | vauthors = Zhang R, Abou-Samra AB | title = Emerging roles of Lipasin as a critical lipid regulator | journal = Biochemical and Biophysical Research Communications | volume = 432 | issue = 3 | pages = 401–405 | date = March 2013 | pmid = 23415864 | doi = 10.1016/j.bbrc.2013.01.129 | bibcode = 2013BBRC..432..401Z }}</ref>

== Function ==
The encoded 22 kDa protein contains an [[N-terminal]] secretion signal and two [[coiled-coil]] domains and is a member of the [[Angiopoietin-like proteins|angiopoietin-like (ANGPTL) protein family]]. However, in contrast to other ANGPTL proteins, ANGPTL8 lacks the C-terminal fibrinogen-like domain, and therefore it is an atypical member of the ANGPTL family.<ref name="pmid23261442">{{cite journal | vauthors = Fu Z, Yao F, Abou-Samra AB, Zhang R | title = Lipasin, thermoregulated in brown fat, is a novel but atypical member of the angiopoietin-like protein family | journal = Biochemical and Biophysical Research Communications | volume = 430 | issue = 3 | pages = 1126–1131 | date = January 2013 | pmid = 23261442 | doi = 10.1016/j.bbrc.2012.12.025 | bibcode = 2013BBRC..430.1126F }}</ref> ANGPTL8 has been shown to form complexes with ANGPTL3 with an apparent stoichiometry of 3:1 of ANGPTL3 to ANGPTL8 respectively.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chen YQ, Pottanat TG, Siegel RW, Ehsani M, Qian YW, Zhen EY, Regmi A, Roell WC, Guo H, Luo MJ, Gimeno RE, Van't Hooft F, Konrad RJ | title = Angiopoietin-like protein 8 differentially regulates ANGPTL3 and ANGPTL4 during postprandial partitioning of fatty acids | journal = Journal of Lipid Research | volume = 61 | issue = 8 | pages = 1203–1220 | date = August 2020 | pmid = 32487544 | pmc = 7397750 | doi = 10.1194/jlr.ra120000781 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Formation of these complexes appears to require intracellular co-folding as mixing of ANGPTL8 and ANGPTL3 extracellularly does not result in complex formation.<ref name=":0">{{cite journal | vauthors = Chi X, Britt EC, Shows HW, Hjelmaas AJ, Shetty SK, Cushing EM, Li W, Dou A, Zhang R, Davies BS | title = ANGPTL8 promotes the ability of ANGPTL3 to bind and inhibit lipoprotein lipase | journal = Molecular Metabolism | volume = 6 | issue = 10 | pages = 1137–1149 | date = October 2017 | pmid = 29031715 | pmc = 5641604 | doi = 10.1016/j.molmet.2017.06.014 }}</ref> ANGPTL8 is expressed in the hepatic tissue and secreted into circulation, in order for the efficient secretion of ANGPTL8 it must form a complex with ANGPTL3.<ref name=":0" /> ANGPTL8 alone shows little inhibitory capacity and must form a complex with [[ANGPTL3]] to inhibit the enzyme [[Lipoprotein lipase]] (LPL) and has been shown to greatly promote the ability of [[ANGPTL3]] to inhibit [[Lipoprotein lipase|LPL]].<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Haller JF, Mintah IJ, Shihanian LM, Stevis P, Buckler D, Alexa-Braun CA, Kleiner S, Banfi S, Cohen JC, Hobbs HH, Yancopoulos GD, Murphy AJ, Gusarova V, Gromada J | title = ANGPTL8 requires ANGPTL3 to inhibit lipoprotein lipase and plasma triglyceride clearance | journal = Journal of Lipid Research | volume = 58 | issue = 6 | pages = 1166–1173 | date = June 2017 | pmid = 28413163 | pmc = 5454515 | doi = 10.1194/jlr.M075689 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In mice ANGPTL8 is secreted by the liver and by adipose tissue, hepatic overexpression of ANGPTL8 causes elevation of circulating [[Triglyceride]] levels.<ref name="pmid22809513"/><ref name="pmid22569073"/>

Despite having elevated post-heparin plasma LPL activity, mice lacking ANGPTL8 exhibit markedly decreased uptake of [[Very low-density lipoprotein]]-derived [[fatty acid]]s into white [[adipose tissue]] (WAT).<ref name="pmid24043787"/> The defect in fatty acids uptake by WAT in ANGPTL8-null mice is likely due to the enhanced fatty acid uptake by the heart and skeletal muscle, because of the elevated LPL activity in these two tissues,<ref name="pmid26687026">{{cite journal | vauthors = Fu Z, Abou-Samra AB, Zhang R | title = A lipasin/Angptl8 monoclonal antibody lowers mouse serum triglycerides involving increased postprandial activity of the cardiac lipoprotein lipase | journal = Scientific Reports | volume = 5 | article-number = 18502 | date = December 2015 | pmid = 26687026 | pmc = 4685196 | doi = 10.1038/srep18502 | bibcode = 2015NatSR...518502F }}</ref> as suggested by the ANGPTL3-4-8 model.<ref name="PMID_27053679">{{cite journal | vauthors = Zhang R | title = The ANGPTL3-4-8 model, a molecular mechanism for triglyceride trafficking | journal = Open Biology | volume = 6 | issue = 4 | article-number = 150272 | date = April 2016 | pmid = 27053679 | pmc = 4852456 | doi = 10.1098/rsob.150272  | doi-access = free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Zhang R, Zhang K|title=An updated ANGPTL3-4-8 model as a mechanism of triglyceride partitioning between fat and oxidative tissues |journal=Progress in Lipid Research |doi=10.1016/j.plipres.2021.101140 |date = Jan 2022|volume=85 |article-number=101140 |pmid=34793860 |pmc=8760165 }}</ref> Sustained ANGPTL3/8 activity, as seen in APOA5 deficiency,<ref>{{cite journal |title=ApoA5 lowers triglyceride levels via suppression of ANGPTL3/8-mediated LPL inhibition |journal=J Lipid Res |date=Mar 2021 |volume=62 |article-number=100068 |doi=10.1016/j.jlr.2021.100068 |pmid=33762177 |pmc=8079461 | vauthors = Chen YQ, Pottanat TG, Zhen EY, Siegel RW, Ehsani M, Qian Y, Konrad RJ }}</ref> leads to reduced LPL abundance and activity in the heart arterial endothelium.<ref>{{cite journal |title=Hypertriglyceridemia in Apoa5-/- mice results from reduced amounts of lipoprotein lipase in the capillary lumen |journal=J Clin Invest |date=Dec 2023 |volume=133 |issue=23 |article-number=e172600 |doi=10.1172/JCI172600 |pmid=37824203 |pmc=10688983 | vauthors = Yang Y, Beigneux AP, Song W, Nguyen LP, Jung H, Tu Y, Weston TA, Tran CM, Xie K, Yu RG, Tran AP, Miyashita K, Nakajima K, Murakami M, Chen YQ, Zhen EY, Kim JR, Kim PH, Birrane G, Tontonoz P, Ploug M, Konrad RJ, Fong LG, Young SG }}</ref>

ANGPTL8 was proposed to increase the rate at which [[beta-cell]]s undergo [[cell division]]. Injection of mice with ANGPTL8 cDNA lowered [[blood sugar]] (i.e. hypoglycemia), presumably due to action at the [[pancreas]]. However, treatment of human islets with ANGPTL8 is unable to increase beta-cell division.<ref name="pmid24353178">{{cite journal | vauthors = Jiao Y, Le Lay J, Yu M, Naji A, Kaestner KH | title = Elevated mouse hepatic betatrophin expression does not increase human β-cell replication in the transplant setting | journal = Diabetes | volume = 63 | issue = 4 | pages = 1283–1288 | date = April 2014 | pmid = 24353178 | pmc = 3964501 | doi = 10.2337/db13-1435 }}</ref> Furthermore, studies in ANGPTL8 knock-out mice do not support a role of ANGPTL8 in controlling beta cell growth, yet point to a clear role in regulating plasma triglyceride levels.<ref name="pmid25417115 ">{{cite journal | vauthors = Gusarova V, Alexa CA, Na E, Stevis PE, Xin Y, Bonner-Weir S, Cohen JC, Hobbs HH, Murphy AJ, Yancopoulos GD, Gromada J | title = ANGPTL8/betatrophin does not control pancreatic beta cell expansion | journal = Cell | volume = 159 | issue = 3 | pages = 691–696 | date = October 2014 | pmid = 25417115 | pmc = 4243040 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2014.09.027 }}</ref> Based on these studies, it is fairly safe to say that the notion that ANGPTL8 promotes beta cell expansion is dead, which was made official by the retraction of the original paper.<ref name="pmid24353178" /><ref name=pmid24651805>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stewart AF | title = Betatrophin versus bitter-trophin and the elephant in the room: time for a new normal in β-cell regeneration research | journal = Diabetes | volume = 63 | issue = 4 | pages = 1198–1199 | date = April 2014 | pmid = 24651805 | pmc = 3964499 | doi = 10.2337/DB14-0009 }}</ref> Deletion of ANGPTL8 does not seem to impact glucose and insulin tolerance in mice.<ref name="pmid24043787">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wang Y, Quagliarini F, Gusarova V, Gromada J, Valenzuela DM, Cohen JC, Hobbs HH | title = Mice lacking ANGPTL8 (Betatrophin) manifest disrupted triglyceride metabolism without impaired glucose homeostasis | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 110 | issue = 40 | pages = 16109–16114 | date = October 2013 | pmid = 24043787 | pmc = 3791734 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.1315292110 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2013PNAS..11016109W }}</ref>

== Structure ==
Three dimensional structure of none of the members of Angiopoietin like proteins (ANGPTLs) is available up until now.{{when|date=May 2022}} However, the structure of ANGPTL8 was predicted by homology modeling and is also reported in literature.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Siddiqa A, Ahmad J, Ali A, Paracha RZ, Bibi Z, Aslam B | title = Structural characterization of ANGPTL8 (betatrophin) with its interacting partner lipoprotein lipase | journal = Computational Biology and Chemistry | volume = 61 | pages = 210–220 | date = April 2016 | pmid = 26908254 | doi = 10.1016/j.compbiolchem.2016.01.009 }}</ref> It consists of alpha helices and its sequence show high similarity with the coiled-coil domains of [[ANGPTL3]] and [[ANGPTL4]].

== Pathway ==
The ANGPTL8 regulatory pathway has been constructed recently by integrating the information of its known transcription factors which is available at WikiPathways data repository with the pathway id '''WP3915'''.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Siddiqa A, Cirillo E, Tareen SH, Ali A, Kutmon M, Eijssen LM, Ahmad J, Evelo CT, Coort SL | title = Visualizing the regulatory role of Angiopoietin-like protein 8 (ANGPTL8) in glucose and lipid metabolic pathways | journal = Genomics | volume = 109 | issue = 5–6 | pages = 408–418 | date = October 2017 | pmid = 28684091 | doi = 10.1016/j.ygeno.2017.06.006 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

== Clinical significance ==
It was hoped that ANGPTL8 or its [[Homology (biology)|homolog]] in humans may provide an effective treatment for [[type 2 diabetes]] and perhaps even [[type I diabetes]].<ref name="pmid23623304 "/> Unfortunately, since new data have greatly called into question the ability of ANGPTL8 to increase beta-cell replication, its potential use as a therapy for type 2 diabetes is limited.<ref name="pmid25417115 "/> Inhibition of ANGPTL8 represents a possible therapeutic strategy for [[hypertriglyceridemia]].<ref name="pmid26687026"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=R |title=The Potential of ANGPTL8 Antagonism to Simultaneously Reduce Triglyceride and Increase HDL-Cholesterol Plasma Levels |journal=Frontiers in Cardiovascular Medicine |date=Nov 2021 |volume=8 |article-number=795370 |doi=10.3389/fcvm.2021.795370 |doi-access=free |pmid=34869703 |pmc=8635044 }}</ref>
In a clinical trial, a human ANGPTL3/8 monoclonal antibody reduced the concentration of triglycerides (-70%), and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (-32%), while increasing HDL- cholesterol (+27%),<ref>{{cite journal |title=Targeting the angiopoietin-like protein 3/8 complex with a monoclonal antibody in patients with mixed hyperlipidemia: a phase 1 trial |journal=Nature Medicine |date=Aug 2025 |volume=31 |issue=8 |pages=2632–2639 |doi=10.1038/s41591-025-03830-4 |pmid=40640392 |pmc=12353791 | vauthors = Gaudet D, Gonciarz M, Shen X, Leohr JK, Beyer TP, Day JW, Mullins GR, Zhen EY, Hartley M, Larouche M, Konrad RJ, Benichou O, Ruotolo G }}</ref> representing a promising therapy for multiple lipid disorders.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=R |title=ANGPTL3/8: one target, multiple lipid disorders |journal=Trends in Molecular Medicine |date=Oct 2025 |doi=10.1016/j.molmed.2025.10.003 |pmid=41168024 |pmc=12614821 |pmc-embargo-date=October 29, 2026 }}</ref>
